draft.introduction: The encounter between Jesus and Nicodemus in John 3:1–8 is the New Testament’s most direct statement about the necessity of radical transformation. Nicodemus—a Pharisee, a ruler, a man of learning—comes to Jesus at night with a polite theological compliment. Jesus ignores the compliment entirely and makes a claim that leaves Nicodemus bewildered: “Unless one is born from above (anothen, ἄνωθεν), one cannot see the kingdom of God.” The Greek word anothen means both “again” and “from above,” and the entire conversation turns on this ambiguity. Nicodemus hears “born again” and asks the obvious biological question. Jesus answers with “born of water and Spirit”—a phrase the Church Fathers universally read as referring to baptism, and which the later theosis tradition read as describing the beginning of human transformation into divine life. The text is from Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: Now there was a man of the Pharisees named Nicodemus, a ruler of the Jews. He came to him at night and said to him, “Rabbi, we know that you have come from God as a teacher, for no one can do these signs that you do unless God is with him.”
translation.original_text: ἦν δὲ ἄνθρωπος ἐκ τῶν Φαρισαίων Νικόδημος ὄνομα αὐτῷ · ἄρχων τῶν Ιουδαίων · οὗτος ἦλθεν προς αὐτὸν νυκτὸς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ · ῥαββεί · οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἀπὸ θεοῦ ἐλήλυθας διδάσκαλος ·

translation.text: Jesus answered and said to him, “Truly, truly, I say to you: unless one is born from above (anothen, ἄνωθεν), one cannot see the kingdom of God.” Nicodemus said to him, “How can a man be born when he is old? Can he enter a second time into his mother’s womb and be born?”
translation.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ιησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ · ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω σοι · ἐὰν μή τις γεννηθῇ ἄνωθεν · οὐ δύναται ἰδεῖν τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ ·

translation.text: Jesus answered, “Truly, truly, I say to you: unless one is born of water and Spirit, one cannot enter the kingdom of God. What is born of the flesh is flesh, and what is born of the Spirit is spirit.”
translation.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ιησοῦς · ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω σοι · ἐὰν μή τις γεννηθῇ ἐξ ὕδατος καὶ πνεύματος · οὐ δύναται εἰσελθεῖν εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ ·

translation.text: “Do not marvel that I said to you: you must be born from above. The wind (pneuma, πνεῦμα) blows where it wills, and you hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it goes. So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit.”
translation.original_text: μὴ θαυμάσῃς ὅτι εἶπον σοι · δεῖ ὑμᾶς γεννηθῆναι ἄνωθεν · τὸ πνεῦμα ὅπου θέλει πνεῖ · καὶ τὴν φωνὴν αὐτοῦ ἀκούεις · ἀλλ’ οὐκ οἶδας πόθεν ἔρχεται καὶ ποῦ ὑπάγει ·

translator_notes.note: The Greek anothen (ἄνωθεν) is deliberately ambiguous: it can mean “again” (as Nicodemus takes it) or “from above” (as Jesus intends it). The entire dialogue is constructed on this double meaning. Nicodemus understands physical rebirth; Jesus means ontological rebirth—birth from a heavenly source. The Church Fathers used this passage as the scriptural foundation for baptismal regeneration. The theosis tradition read it more radically: being “born from above” means receiving a divine nature, becoming a participant in the life of God.

The Greek pneuma (πνεῦμα) means both “wind” and “spirit,” and Jesus exploits the ambiguity just as he did with anothen. The Spirit, like the wind, is invisible, unpredictable, and sovereign. You cannot control it or comprehend its origin and destination. This is a direct challenge to Nicodemus’s Pharisaic assumption that relationship with God can be managed through knowledge of the law. The Spirit blows where it wills—not where the theologian expects.
